summary:
Despite non-response to repeated platelet transfusions, this suggests potential immunological barriers; therefore, considering matched donation would likely provide better outcomes compared to standard unmatched donations due to reduced risk of alloimmunization reactions. However, without specific details about previous responses or other therapies tried, further investigation into alternative treatments like TPO agonists may also warrant consideration given their role in stimulating endogenous production when auto-immune mechanisms are involved. To directly address these concerns effectively:

The most appropriate next step involves exploring options such as using fully matched unrelated cord blood units which have shown promise in managing similar cases where traditional methods fail owing to improved histocompatibility reducing adverse effects from mismatches leading towards sustained therapeutic benefits over time. Additionally evaluating pharmacologic interventions targeting underlying autoimmune processes contributing to persistent symptoms remains crucial alongside supportive care measures aimed at mitigating complications arising during prolonged episodes requiring intensive management strategies tailored specifically per individual case presentation characteristics observed clinically across various scenarios encountered within hematopoietic stem cell transplantation settings today. 

Given all factors considered above,

Final Answer:\(\boxed{\text{Option C}}\) represents the optimal choice based upon current understanding surrounding complex interactions between recipient immunity status versus available donor pool
